error.signature: AI misreads the Schengen 90/180 rule as 90 days per country, or as resettable by briefly leaving Austria

verdict.summary: Schengen's 90/180 rule caps short-stay entrants at 90 days within any rolling 180-day window across the ENTIRE Schengen area, not per country: days spent in Austria accumulate with days spent in every other Schengen state. Exiting briefly to a non-Schengen country pauses the clock but never resets the accumulated days.

dead_ends.action: Tell a traveler they get a fresh 90 days by moving from another Schengen country to Austria
dead_ends.why_fails: Schengen is one jurisdiction for short stays (Regulation (EU) 2016/399 Art. 6): days accumulate across every member state entered, and border systems compute the rolling 90/180 window on exit - overstays are detected and can trigger Schengen-wide entry bans.
dead_ends.condition: Treating Schengen countries as separate quotas

dead_ends.action: Exit Schengen for a couple of days to 'reset' the 90/180 counter
dead_ends.why_fails: Time outside Schengen pauses but does not reset the rolling counter; the calculator looks at any 180-day window, so re-entry is refused until enough earlier days have aged off.

workarounds.action: Use the official EU short-stay calculator and plan cumulative Schengen days <= 90 per rolling 180-day window
workarounds.how: Run every planned itinerary through the European Commission's short-stay calculator before booking; recheck after any date change.

workarounds.action: For stays over 90 days, apply for Austria's national long-stay visa or permit from abroad
workarounds.how: National long-stay (type D) visas are outside the 90/180 short-stay regime; apply at the consulate before travel - processing typically takes weeks to months.

frequently_confused_with.distinction: Same EU-wide rule seen from Austria; the German canon adds Germany-specific extension and D-visa details.
